chain_of_thought: Ghidra MCP backend is not reachable, so I cannot decompile or inspect native functions; similarly I cannot navigate to specific functions like strlen_default or the JNI stub.

Given only the high-level crash info, I must reason generically: crash in strlen_default usually indicates passing a non-terminated or invalid pointer to strlen. In JNI Base64__decode, typical cause is treating arbitrary byte data as C-string without length bounds.
The stack shows strlen_default called from Java_com_dreamsecurity_dstoolkit_util_Base64__1decode; no allocator/sanitizer frames, so the first app-native frame is exactly that JNI decode function.

Without code, I can’t prove whether the pointer given to strlen is derived directly from attacker-controlled Java String/byte[] without validation, but the context (Base64 decode, JNI fuzz target, app crypto helpers) strongly suggests inputs ultimately come from untrusted data in realistic use (crypto / Base64 on arbitrary payloads).

Fuzzer likely passed malformed or non-UTF-8 / non-null-terminated data into the JNI layer, triggering strlen on a bad pointer or on an unterminated buffer. This is a common real-world bug pattern: assuming a buffer is null-terminated when it’s not, or using strlen on data whose size is tracked separately.

strlen out-of-bounds read is a memory safety issue (CWE-125) and can be used for information disclosure or to lead to further corruption (if its result feeds into malloc/memcpy). Even if only read, it’s still a security bug if reachable with attacker data.

However, I lack direct proof that in the production app the same code is reachable with attacker-controlled data of arbitrary content; but Base64 and AES helpers are typically exposed to user / network data paths, making it plausible.
Given this, I classify as likely genuine vulnerability (unsafe C-string handling on untrusted data) but with moderate, not full, confidence due to missing decompilation evidence.
Severity: at least medium since it is a native memory read bug that can cause DoS and potential info leak; absent proof of write or control-flow, I won’t rate it high/critical.
reasons: Crash occurs inside strlen_default called from app JNI Base64 decode function, indicating strlen was used on an invalid or non-null-terminated pointer.
The JNI Base64__decode is directly reachable from Java aes_decode(), which processes external data, so inputs can plausibly be attacker-controlled.
Misuse of C string APIs on untrusted buffers is a classic memory safety issue (out-of-bounds read / information leak / DoS).

evidence.note: Top native frame where process terminated; indicates traversal past valid buffer when computing string length.
Direct caller of strlen_default in the stack; likely passes attacker-derived data as C-string without ensuring null-termination.
Java bridge method invoking JNI Base64 decode; reachable from application thread S01$Thread_screen_capture_path$1.run().

recommendations: Review Java_com_dreamsecurity_dstoolkit_util_Base64__1decode implementation to ensure it never passes unvalidated or non-null-terminated data to strlen; use length-tracked APIs (memchr, explicit length fields) instead of C-string functions.
Add explicit bounds checks and validation for all inputs arriving from Java (String / byte[]) before converting to native pointers.
Re-run fuzzing with ASan/UBSan on the native libs to confirm and better localize the exact unsafe call sequence (strlen and any subsequent allocations/memcpy).
assumptions: Java aes_decode() and the JNI Base64 decode are used with attacker-influenced data in realistic application flows.
The crash in strlen_default is due to out-of-bounds read from improper C-string handling rather than a pure harness artifact like passing a deliberately invalid pointer.
limitations: Unable to decompile native functions due to MCP backend connection failure, so reasoning is based only on stack trace and typical patterns.
No direct view of how Java strings/arrays are marshalled into native code or whether additional validation occurs.
